narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which stands out positively above the typical 3.21 average, along with your plans to attend full-time like most applicants and a middle family income bracket. The admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated your risk of failure at 41%—a level high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar success. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your background, with your undergraduate GPA emerging as the most influential factor, strongly pushing the model toward expecting you to pass rather than fail. Next in importance was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is well below the average of around 5.5 and significantly nudged the prediction toward a higher failure risk, signaling weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Your family income bracket played a smaller but still notable role, leaning slightly in favor of passing by aligning with typical successful applicants. Compared to the broader group of students in the model's data, your low Year 3 decile stands out as unusually challenging, while your solid GPA offers a counterbalance that wasn't quite enough to offset it. The LSAT score of 20, far below the average of 36.7, likely contributed indirectly through the overall profile but wasn't among the top drivers here. This decision reflects how the model weighs early academic struggles more heavily for bar predictions, even for determined full-time students like you. While disappointing, this rejection doesn't define your potential—many in similar situations have improved through targeted efforts. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average, seeking academic advising to strengthen future decile rankings, and exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your Hispanic background and resilience. Building a stronger narrative around your GPA's strengths could also help in reapplying or appealing. With focused steps, you're well-positioned to turn this around.
